concept_name: Torque on a Current Loop in a Magnetic Field (τ = μ × B)
source_book: DC Pandey Vol 2 Ch.26 §26.6 — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. Diamond #3 of the magnetism chapter (phase M2 per docs/MAGNETISM_ARCHITECTURE.md §10, archetype C — closed-loop rotational dynamics). The atomic concept being taught is that a current loop in a uniform B feels zero net force but a non-zero torque τ = μ × B, where μ = NIA points perpendicular to the loop face (RHR-derived from current direction). The four most common student errors targeted here: (a) believing magnetic force does work on the loop, (b) believing the net force on a rotating loop is non-zero, (c) believing torque is maximum when μ ∥ B (alignment), (d) confusing the RHR for μ with the RHR for the current's own field. Diamond #3 establishes archetype-C renderer primitives (rectangular loop, force-pair animation, μ vector through loop face, τ vector along rotation axis, oscillation animation, loop↔bar-magnet swap) so vibration_magnetometer, moving_coil_galvanometer, and bar_magnet_in_uniform_field can author against them via the M3 patterns library. Conforms to Rule 20 M1-M6 exception: ships conceptual-only; mode_overrides.board deferred to M7, .competitive deferred to M8.

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the analog galvanometer in your school physics lab — the brass-framed instrument with a thin needle that flicks across a paper scale when current flows. Inside that brass case is a tiny rectangular coil suspended in the field of two permanent magnets. When you connect a battery, current flows through the coil and the same τ = μ × B torque you'll see in a moment swings the needle. The bigger the current, the bigger the torque, the further the needle deflects against the restoring spring. Every micrometer reading on that scale is a torque measurement.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture a ceiling fan motor in a Bengaluru summer apartment. Inside the white plastic housing is a rotor wound with current-carrying coils sitting in the field of the stator's permanent magnets. Each half-rotation, the commutator flips the current direction so the torque on the rotor stays in the same rotational sense. That continuous τ = μ × B torque, flipped at the right moment, is what keeps the blades spinning at 350 RPM all afternoon.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture a moving-coil microphone on stage at an IIT-Bombay Mood Indigo concert. The singer's voice vibrates a thin diaphragm, which vibrates a tiny coil sitting in a permanent-magnet field. Each oscillation of the coil is a τ = μ × B torque cycle. The induced EMF reads out the audio signal. The microphone is just a galvanometer running backwards.

N.name: Number of turns in the coil

L_side.name: Side length of the square loop

B.name: Magnitude of the uniform external field

tau_magnitude.formula: N * I * (L_side * L_side) * B * sin(theta_deg * PI / 180)

formulas.tau_vector: τ = μ × B — the vector torque is the cross product of the loop's magnetic moment with the external field.
formulas.tau_magnitude: τ = μ B sin θ — scalar magnitude; θ is the angle between μ and B; τ is maximum at θ = 90° and zero at θ = 0° or 180°.
formulas.mu_definition: μ = N I A — the magnetic moment of the loop. Direction: perpendicular to the loop face, RHR (curl fingers along current, thumb gives μ).
formulas.net_force_zero: F_top + F_bottom + F_left + F_right = 0 — opposite sides feel equal-and-opposite forces; the loop translates nowhere.
formulas.force_per_side: F = I (L × B) — force on each current-carrying segment of length L.
formulas.equilibrium_stable: θ = 0° (μ ∥ B) — stable equilibrium; small perturbation returns to alignment.
formulas.equilibrium_unstable: θ = 180° (μ anti-parallel to B) — unstable; any perturbation grows.
formulas.no_work_on_loop: Pure magnetic torque is conservative on the loop face. KE oscillates symmetrically about θ = 0 with no average gain — magnetic field does no NET work on the rotating loop.

constraints: B must be uniform across the loop. Non-uniform B adds a net force (gradient term) and changes the dynamics.
Loop is rigid — wire is non-deformable; geometry preserved during rotation.
Rotation axis is fixed by the geometry — the axis perpendicular to BOTH the field-aligned sides and the τ vector.
θ is measured from μ to B (not B to μ). Sign convention: positive θ → restoring torque toward θ = 0.
τ = μ × B holds for ANY planar loop shape (rectangle, circle, triangle); the rectangular case is the easy derivation, not a special case.
No emf back-reaction included here — induction belongs to the next chapter.

aha_moment.statement: ΣF can be zero while Στ is non-zero — rotation without translation.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: Watch STATE_3 → STATE_4: ΣF arrow shrinks to a literal zero badge, yet the τ vector appears and the loop starts to rotate. The two facts are simultaneously true.

STATE_1.title: Setup — a rectangular loop sits at θ = 90° in a uniform B field

scene_composition.text: A rectangular current loop in uniform B (θ = 90°)

Blue arrows = B (uniform field)
Yellow loop = current-carrying coil
Orange dots = current flowing around coil
Watch the dots — they show which way I flows.

Question:
What happens to the loop?
Same setup as the school-lab galvanometer — coil suspended in a permanent-magnet field.

tts_sentences.text_en: Setup. Blue arrows are the uniform magnetic field B.
The yellow rectangle is a current loop. Watch the orange dots — they flow around the loop showing you exactly which way the current moves.
The loop sits at theta equals ninety — its face is parallel to B.
Question. What does the loop do? Does it slide? Does it spin? Does it just sit there?

STATE_2.title: Force on one side — F = I L × B reveals on the left side

scene_composition.text: Predict, then reveal: F on the LEFT side

Watch the dots on the LEFT side.
Current flows UP.
B points to the right.
Predict: which way is the force?

See the guide (top right):
I arrow = up, B arrow = right
→ F = I × B points INTO the page.
F₁ into the page. ✓
F₁ = I · L_side · B. For our defaults: F₁ = 0.5 · 0.10 · 0.1 = 0.005 N.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the orange dots on the left side of the loop. They flow upward. Current on the left side is going up.
Predict before I show you. Current up, field pointing right. Which way is the force? Take three seconds.
Look at the guide in the upper right. Three arrows: I pointing up, B pointing right, and F equals I cross B.
F points into the page — away from you. That is the cross product: thumb up, fingers right, palm faces away. F-one into the page.
Magnitude: I times L times B. For our defaults, five thousandths of a newton.

STATE_3.title: Force on the opposite side — F₂ is anti-parallel; ΣF = 0

scene_composition.text: Force on the RIGHT side — and net force

Watch the dots on the RIGHT side.
Current flows DOWN.
B still points right.
Predict F before looking at the guide.

Guide shows: I down, B right
→ F = I × B points OUT of the page.
F₂ and F₁ are anti-parallel. ✓
ΣF = F₁ + F₂ = 0. The loop will NOT translate. Anywhere.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the right side. Watch the orange dots — they flow downward. Current on the right is going down.
Predict again before the guide shows you. Current down, field right. Which way is the force?
Look at the guide. I arrow down, B arrow right. F equals I cross B — out of the page this time.
F-two is out of the page. Opposite direction to F-one. Same magnitude. Add them — net force is zero.
But notice — the forces act on DIFFERENT lines of action. Left side and right side. That asymmetry is about to matter.

STATE_4.title: Torque emerges — two opposite forces on different lines = a couple

scene_composition.text: A couple → rotation, not translation

Two equal-and-opposite forces
on different lines of action
= COUPLE

ΣF = 0  ✓  no translation
Στ ≠ 0  →  rotation begins
Zero net force and non-zero net torque are simultaneously true. This is the heart of rotational dynamics.

tts_sentences.text_en: Two forces. Equal magnitude. Opposite direction. Different lines of action. What does that combination do?
Take three seconds. Picture the loop. Picture the forces.
It is a couple. A couple produces rotation with no translation. The loop will spin about the vertical axis through its centre.
Sum of forces is zero. Sum of torques is not zero. Both statements are true at the same time.
This is the heart of rotational dynamics. Forces decide translation. Torques decide rotation.

misconception_watch.belief: If ΣF = 0, the loop must be in equilibrium and cannot move at all.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Translational equilibrium ≠ rotational equilibrium. A couple has ΣF = 0 but Στ ≠ 0 — the loop rotates but does not translate.

STATE_5.title: The dipole moment μ = NIA — direction by RHR through the loop face

scene_composition.text: The magnetic moment μ = N I A

μ is a VECTOR.
Magnitude: N · I · A
Direction: ⊥ loop face
(RHR: curl fingers along I,
 thumb gives μ).

μ-arrow length scales as N · I · A.
Double the turns → double μ.
Double the current → double μ.
Double the area → double μ.
μ unifies the loop with the bar magnet. A current loop IS a magnetic dipole. STATE 7 will make the analogy visual.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now name the thing that makes a loop magnetic. It is called the magnetic moment, written as mu.
Magnitude: N times I times A. N is the number of turns, I is the current, A is the loop area.
Direction: perpendicular to the loop face. Right-hand rule: curl your fingers in the direction of current. Your thumb points along mu.
Watch the mu-arrow size. It scales with N times I times A. Bigger turns or bigger current means bigger mu.

STATE_6.title: τ = μ B sin θ — sweep θ from 0 to 180° and watch the torque

scene_composition.text: Watch θ sweep 0 → 180° — τ scales as μ B sin θ

Predict: at what angle
is the torque MAXIMUM?

θ = 0?  θ = 90?  θ = 180?

Answer: θ = 90°.
τ = μ B sin 90° = μ B
(max)

At θ = 0 and 180°, τ = 0.
Those are equilibria.
θ = 0°: μ ∥ B — STABLE equilibrium. θ = 180°: μ anti-parallel to B — UNSTABLE. A small nudge here grows, and the loop flips through 180°.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the loop. Theta sweeps from zero up to one-eighty and back, on a ten-second cycle.
Predict first. At what angle is the torque maximum? Take three seconds.
Common wrong guess: mu parallel to B. Many students believe alignment means maximum.
Actual answer: theta equals ninety degrees. Sine ninety is one — that is when the cross product is largest.
At theta zero, mu is parallel to B — sine is zero — torque is zero. No turning effort. That is an equilibrium.
Theta zero is stable. Theta one-eighty is also an equilibrium, but unstable. Any small perturbation grows — the loop flips.

misconception_watch.belief: Torque is maximum when μ ∥ B because that is when the dipole is most aligned.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Alignment means τ = 0, not τ_max. τ = μ × B is a cross product — it is biggest when μ ⊥ B.

STATE_7.title: Loop ↔ bar magnet — same μ, same physics, oscillation interactive

scene_composition.text: A current loop IS a bar magnet

Watch:
Loop fades →  bar magnet

μ vector stays put.
Torque stays the same.

The dipole oscillates about
θ = 0 (stable equilibrium).

μ ∥ B is the resting pose —
just like a compass needle.
Same τ = μ × B for both. The galvanometer needle (bar magnet) and the motor rotor (current loop) live in the same equation.

tts_sentences.text_en: Final move. Watch — the loop fades into a bar magnet with the same mu direction. Red end is N, blue end is S.
Notice. Torque on the bar magnet is the same as torque on the loop. The two are physically identical.
The dipole oscillates back and forth around theta equals zero — exactly like a compass needle in a field.
This is why a galvanometer needle deflects. This is why a motor rotor spins. Same physics: tau equals mu cross B.

epic_c_branches.misconception: The magnetic force does work on the current loop — it speeds up the rotation, so KE rises.
trigger_phrases: magnetic force speeds up the loop
B does work on the rotating loop
loop rotation gains energy from B

epic_c_branches.misconception: A rotating loop must have a net force pushing it sideways — rotation cannot happen without translation force.
trigger_phrases: net force on the loop is nonzero
ΣF cannot be zero while spinning

epic_c_branches.misconception: Torque is maximum when μ is parallel to B — alignment means maximum effect.
trigger_phrases: torque max when mu parallel to B
tau is largest when mu aligned

epic_c_branches.misconception: The right-hand rule for μ is the same as the right-hand rule for the magnetic field around a current — fingers along the current, thumb along μ.
trigger_phrases: mu direction by RHR for wire
RHR for mu same as B around wire

STATE_1.label: Setup — loop at θ = 90°, no motion
STATE_1.caption: Rectangular loop in uniform B. θ = 90° — loop face parallel to the field axis. No force vectors shown yet.

STATE_2.label: F₁ on the left side — RHR reveals F₁ out of page
STATE_2.caption: Left side: current up, B right. RHR → F₁ INTO page. Magnitude I·L·B.

STATE_3.label: F₂ on the right — anti-parallel — ΣF = 0
STATE_3.caption: Right side: current down, B right. RHR → F₂ OUT of page. F₁ + F₂ = 0. Loop will not translate.

STATE_4.label: Torque emerges — couple → rotation about vertical axis
STATE_4.caption: ΣF = 0 yet Στ ≠ 0. The couple rotates the loop about the vertical axis. τ-arrow appears along the rotation axis.

STATE_5.label: μ vector through loop face — N, I, L sliders live
STATE_5.caption: μ = NIA. Direction: perpendicular to loop face, RHR (fingers curl with current, thumb gives μ). Sliders scale μ.

STATE_6.label: θ sweep 0→180° — τ = μBsinθ visible
STATE_6.caption: Drag θ slider. Watch τ-arrow scale with sinθ. Max at 90°. Zero at 0° and 180°. Stable vs unstable equilibrium revealed.

STATE_7.label: Loop ↔ bar magnet swap, oscillation animation
STATE_7.caption: Toggle: loop becomes bar magnet, same μ. Drag to perturb, release to oscillate about θ = 0.
